---
title: "Decision Tree Statistical Analyses in R"
subtitle: "Example R code linked to the prepared datasets"
author: "Darias Holgado & Corinna Martarelli"
output:
  rmdformats::readthedown:
    highlight: kate
    code_folding: hide
   
---

```{r setup}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  error = TRUE
)

data_dir <- file.path("..", "data", "examples")

read_example <- function(file) {
  read.csv(file.path(data_dir, file), stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
}

has_pkg <- function(package) {
  requireNamespace(package, quietly = TRUE)
}

optional_packages <- c(
  "ARTool",
  "bootnet",
  "caret",
  "e1071",
  "lavaan",
  "lme4",
  "lmerTest",
  "MASS",
  "metafor",
  "nnet",
  "ordinal",
  "performance",
  "psych",
  "qgraph",
  "randomForest",
  "rpart"
)

install_missing_packages <- function(packages = optional_packages) {
  missing_packages <- packages[!vapply(packages, has_pkg, logical(1))]

  if (length(missing_packages) == 0) {
    message("All optional packages are already installed.")
    return(invisible(character()))
  }

  install.packages(
    missing_packages,
    repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org"
  )

  invisible(missing_packages)
}
```

## Optional packages

Run this chunk once if you want to install only the packages that are missing on your computer.

```{r install-missing-packages, eval=FALSE}
install_missing_packages()
```

# Associations and relationships

## Pearson correlation

Purpose: Use Pearson correlation to quantify the strength and direction of a linear association between two continuous variables.

```{r pearson-correlation}
data <- read_example("pearson.csv")
cor.test(data$x, data$y, method = "pearson")
plot(data$x, data$y, xlab = "x", ylab = "y")
```

## Spearman rank correlation

Purpose: Use Spearman correlation to assess whether two variables have a monotonic association based on ranks, especially when normality or linearity is not plausible.

```{r spearman-correlation}
data <- read_example("spearman.csv")
cor.test(data$stress_rank, data$wellbeing_rank, method = "spearman", exact = FALSE)
```

## Kendall rank correlation

Purpose: Use Kendall correlation to evaluate rank-order association, often with smaller samples or many tied ranks.

```{r kendall-correlation}
data <- read_example("kendall.csv")
cor.test(data$adherence_rank, data$wellbeing_rank, method = "kendall", exact = FALSE)
```

## Chi-square test of association

Purpose: Use this test to examine whether two categorical variables are statistically associated in a contingency table.

```{r chi-square-association}
data <- read_example("chiSquareAssociation.csv")
tab <- table(data$group, data$outcome)
tab
chisq.test(tab)
```

## Fisher exact test

Purpose: Use Fisher's exact test to examine association between two categorical variables when sample sizes or expected cell counts are small.

```{r fisher-exact}
data <- read_example("fisher.csv")
tab <- table(data$treatment, data$response)
tab
fisher.test(tab)
```

## McNemar test

Purpose: Use McNemar's test to compare paired binary responses, such as before-after classifications from the same participants.

```{r mcnemar-test}
data <- read_example("mcnemar.csv")
tab <- table(data$before, data$after)
tab
mcnemar.test(tab)
```

## Log-linear model

Purpose: Use a log-linear model to analyse associations among several categorical variables through their frequency counts.

```{r log-linear-model}
data <- read_example("logLinearModel.csv")
tab <- xtabs(count ~ therapy + outcome + gender, data = data)

if (has_pkg("MASS")) {
  fit <- MASS::loglm(~ therapy * outcome + gender, data = tab)
  summary(fit)
} else {
  message("Install MASS to run MASS::loglm().")
}
```

## Path analysis / mediation

Purpose: Use path analysis or mediation to evaluate a theoretically specified indirect pathway between measured variables. This can estimate indirect effects, but causal interpretation still depends on design and assumptions.

```{r path-analysis}
data <- read_example("pathAnalysis.csv")

if (has_pkg("lavaan")) {
  model <- '
    mediator ~ a * predictor
    outcome ~ b * mediator + c_prime * predictor
    indirect := a * b
    total := c_prime + indirect
  '

  fit <- lavaan::sem(model, data = data, se = "bootstrap", bootstrap = 5000)
  summary(fit, standardized = TRUE, fit.measures = TRUE, rsquare = TRUE,ci = TRUE)
} else {
  message("Install lavaan to run the mediation model.")
}

#Extract path coefficients

lavaan::parameterEstimates(
  fit,
  standardized = TRUE,
  ci = TRUE
)

#Extract regression path
pe <- lavaan::parameterEstimates(
  fit,
  standardized = TRUE,
  ci = TRUE
)

pe[pe$op == "~", ]

#R2

lavaan::inspect(fit, "r2")

```

## Structural equation modeling

Purpose: Use SEM to test models that combine latent variables, measurement models, and structural relations among variables.

```{r structural-equation-modeling}
data <- read_example("structuralEquationModeling.csv")

if (has_pkg("lavaan")) {
  model <- '
    stress =~ stress1 + stress2 + stress3
    recovery =~ sleep1 + sleep2 + sleep3
    recovery ~ stress
    performance ~ recovery + stress
  '

  fit <- lavaan::sem(model, data = data)
  summary(fit, standardized = TRUE, fit.measures = TRUE, rsquare = TRUE)
} else {
  message("Install lavaan to run SEM.")
}

pe <- lavaan::parameterEstimates(
  fit,
  standardized = TRUE,
  ci = TRUE
)

pe


```

## Confirmatory factor analysis

Purpose: Use confirmatory factor analysis to test whether observed indicators fit a measurement model defined before looking at the data. CFA is appropriate when the expected factors and item-factor assignments are theory-driven.

```{r confirmatory-factor-analysis}
data <- read_example("confirmatoryFactorAnalysis.csv")

if (has_pkg("lavaan")) {
  model <- '
    stress =~ stress1 + stress2 + stress3
    recovery =~ recovery1 + recovery2 + recovery3
    confidence =~ conf1 + conf2 + conf3
  '

  fit <- lavaan::cfa(model, data = data)
  summary(fit, standardized = TRUE, fit.measures = TRUE)
  lavaan::fitMeasures(fit, c("cfi", "tli", "rmsea", "srmr"))
} else {
  message("Install lavaan to run CFA.")
}
```

## Discriminant analysis

Purpose: Use discriminant analysis to classify cases into known groups using continuous predictors and to describe which predictors separate the groups.

```{r discriminant-analysis}
data <- read_example("discriminantAnalysis.csv")
data$group <- factor(data$group)

if (has_pkg("MASS")) {
  fit <- MASS::lda(group ~ anxiety + motivation + reaction_time, data = data)
  fit
  pred <- predict(fit)$class
  table(Predicted = pred, Observed = data$group)
  mean(pred == data$group)
} else {
  message("Install MASS to run linear discriminant analysis.")
}
```

# One-sample analyses

## One-sample t-test

Purpose: Use a one-sample t-test to compare the mean of one continuous variable against a theoretical or reference value.

```{r one-sample-t-test}
data <- read_example("oneSampleT.csv")
t.test(data$score, mu = 0)
```

## One-sample z-test

Purpose: Use a one-sample z-test to compare a sample mean against a reference mean when the population standard deviation is known.

```{r one-sample-z-test}
data <- read_example("oneSampleZ.csv")

mu0 <- 50
sigma <- 15
x <- data$score
n <- length(x)
z <- (mean(x) - mu0) / (sigma / sqrt(n))
p <- 2 * pnorm(-abs(z))

c(
  sample_mean = mean(x),
  null_mean = mu0,
  population_sd = sigma,
  n = n,
  z = z,
  p = p
)
```

## One-sample Wilcoxon signed-rank test

Purpose: Use the one-sample Wilcoxon signed-rank test to compare a median or typical value against a reference value when a parametric t-test is not appropriate.

```{r one-sample-wilcoxon}
data <- read_example("oneSampleWilcoxon.csv")
wilcox.test(data$rating, mu = 0)
```

# Two-group and paired analyses

## Independent-samples t-test

Purpose: Use an independent-samples t-test to compare the means of two independent groups on a continuous outcome.

```{r independent-t-test}
data <- read_example("independentT.csv")
data$group <- factor(data$group)
t.test(score ~ group, data = data, var.equal = FALSE)
```

## Mann-Whitney U test

Purpose: Use the Mann-Whitney U test to compare two independent groups when the outcome is ordinal or when parametric assumptions are not suitable.

```{r mann-whitney-u}
data <- read_example("mannWhitney.csv")
data$group <- factor(data$group)
wilcox.test(pain_score ~ group, data = data)
```

## Paired-samples t-test

Purpose: Use a paired-samples t-test to compare two related measurements, such as pre-post scores from the same participants.

```{r paired-t-test}
data <- read_example("pairedT.csv")
t.test(data$baseline_score, data$followup_score, paired = TRUE)
shapiro.test(data$difference)
```

## Wilcoxon signed-rank test

Purpose: Use the Wilcoxon signed-rank test to compare two paired measurements when the differences are ordinal or non-normal.

```{r wilcoxon-signed-rank}
data <- read_example("wilcoxon.csv")
wilcox.test(data$before, data$after, paired = TRUE)
```

# Variance tests

## Chi-square test for one variance

Purpose: Use this test to compare a sample variance with a hypothesized population variance under normality assumptions.

```{r chi-square-variance}
data <- read_example("chiSquareVariance.csv")

n <- length(data$score)
s2 <- var(data$score)
sigma2_0 <- 100
chi2 <- (n - 1) * s2 / sigma2_0
p <- 2 * min(pchisq(chi2, df = n - 1), 1 - pchisq(chi2, df = n - 1))

c(
  chi_square = chi2,
  df = n - 1,
  p = p
)
```

## F-test for two variances

Purpose: Use the F-test to compare whether two independent groups have equal variances.

```{r f-test-variance}
data <- read_example("varianceFTest.csv")
data$group <- factor(data$group)
var.test(score ~ group, data = data)
```

# ANOVA and nonparametric alternatives

## One-way ANOVA

Purpose: Use one-way ANOVA to compare the means of three or more independent groups defined by one factor.

```{r one-way-anova}
data <- read_example("anova.csv")
data$group <- factor(data$group)

fit <- aov(score ~ group, data = data)
summary(fit)
TukeyHSD(fit)
```

## ANCOVA

Purpose: Use ANCOVA to compare group means on one continuous outcome while adjusting for one or more continuous covariates. It is useful when groups may differ on a baseline score or another variable that should be statistically controlled.

```{r ancova}
data <- read_example("ancova.csv")
data$group <- factor(data$group)

fit <- lm(post_anxiety ~ group + baseline_anxiety, data = data)
anova(fit)
drop1(fit, test = "F")
```

## MANOVA

Purpose: Use MANOVA when you want to compare two or more related continuous dependent variables across groups at the same time. It first tests whether the groups differ on the combined outcome profile before considering any follow-up tests for individual outcomes.

```{r manova}
data <- read_example("manova.csv")
data$group <- factor(data$group)

fit <- manova(
  cbind(stress_score, wellbeing_score, performance_score) ~ group,
  data = data
)
summary(fit, test = "Pillai")
summary(fit, test = "Wilks")
summary.aov(fit)
```

## MANCOVA

Purpose: Use MANCOVA when groups are compared on two or more related continuous dependent variables while statistically controlling one or more continuous covariates. It is useful when baseline differences or participant characteristics should be adjusted before interpreting group differences.

```{r mancova}
data <- read_example("mancova.csv")
data$group <- factor(data$group)

fit <- manova(
  cbind(stress_score, wellbeing_score, performance_score) ~ group + age_years + baseline_score,
  data = data
)
summary(fit, test = "Pillai")
summary(fit, test = "Wilks")
summary.aov(fit)
```

## Repeated-measures ANOVA

Purpose: Use repeated-measures ANOVA to compare means across three or more related conditions or time points from the same participants.

```{r repeated-measures-anova}
data <- read_example("repeatedAnova.csv")

long_data <- data.frame(
  id = rep(data$id, each = 3),
  condition = factor(rep(c("baseline", "week4", "week8"), times = nrow(data))),
  score = c(t(data[, c("baseline", "week4", "week8")]))
)

fit <- aov(score ~ condition + Error(id / condition), data = long_data)
summary(fit)
```

## Two-way ANOVA

Purpose: Use two-way ANOVA to test the main and interaction effects of two or more independent factors on a continuous outcome.

```{r two-way-anova}
data <- read_example("twoWayAnova.csv")
data$therapy <- factor(data$therapy)
data$age_group <- factor(data$age_group)

fit <- aov(stress_score ~ therapy * age_group, data = data)
summary(fit)
```

## Two-way repeated-measures ANOVA

Purpose: Use two-way repeated-measures ANOVA to test main and interaction effects when the same participants are measured across combinations of repeated factors.

```{r two-way-repeated-anova}
data <- read_example("twoWayRepeatedAnova.csv")

long_data <- data.frame(
  participant = rep(data$participant, each = 6),
  load = factor(rep(rep(c("low", "high"), each = 3), times = nrow(data))),
  time = factor(rep(c("pre", "post", "followup"), times = 2 * nrow(data))),
  score = c(t(data[, c("low_pre", "low_post", "low_followup", "high_pre", "high_post", "high_followup")]))
)

fit <- aov(score ~ load * time + Error(participant / (load * time)), data = long_data)
summary(fit)
```

## Kruskal-Wallis test

Purpose: Use the Kruskal-Wallis test to compare three or more independent groups when the outcome is ordinal or parametric assumptions are not suitable.

```{r kruskal-wallis}
data <- read_example("kruskalWallis.csv")
data$group <- factor(data$group)
kruskal.test(rank_score ~ group, data = data)
```

## Friedman test

Purpose: Use the Friedman test to compare three or more related conditions when repeated-measures ANOVA assumptions are not suitable.

```{r friedman-test}
data <- read_example("friedman.csv")
friedman.test(as.matrix(data[, c("condition_a", "condition_b", "condition_c")]))
```

## Nonparametric two-way ANOVA via aligned rank transform

Purpose: Use aligned rank transform ANOVA to test factorial effects with nonparametric data while retaining an ANOVA-like interaction framework.

```{r nonparametric-two-way-anova}
data <- read_example("nonparametricTwoWayAnova.csv")
data$therapy <- factor(data$therapy)
data$age_group <- factor(data$age_group)

if (has_pkg("ARTool")) {
  fit <- ARTool::art(stress_rating ~ therapy * age_group, data = data)
  anova(fit)
} else {
  message("Install ARTool to run aligned rank transform ANOVA.")
}
```

## Nonparametric two-way repeated-measures ANOVA via aligned rank transform

Purpose: Use aligned rank transform methods for repeated factorial designs when the outcome does not meet parametric assumptions.

```{r nonparametric-two-way-repeated-anova}
data <- read_example("nonparametricTwoWayRepeatedAnova.csv")

long_data <- data.frame(
  participant = factor(rep(data$participant, each = 6)),
  load = factor(rep(rep(c("low", "high"), each = 3), times = nrow(data))),
  time = factor(rep(c("pre", "post", "followup"), times = 2 * nrow(data))),
  stress_rating = c(t(data[, c("low_pre", "low_post", "low_followup", "high_pre", "high_post", "high_followup")]))
)

if (has_pkg("ARTool")) {
  fit <- ARTool::art(stress_rating ~ load * time + Error(participant), data = long_data)
  anova(fit)
} else {
  message("Install ARTool to run aligned rank transform ANOVA.")
}
```

# Frequencies and distributions

## Binomial test

Purpose: Use the binomial test to compare an observed number of successes in a two-level outcome with an expected probability.

```{r binomial-test}
data <- read_example("binomialTest.csv")
successes <- sum(data$response == "yes")
n <- length(data$response)
binom.test(successes, n, p = 0.50)
```

## Chi-square goodness-of-fit test

Purpose: Use the chi-square goodness-of-fit test to compare observed category frequencies with expected frequencies from a theoretical distribution.

```{r chi-square-goodness}
data <- read_example("chiSquareGoodness.csv")
observed <- table(data$preference)
observed
chisq.test(observed, p = rep(1 / length(observed), length(observed)))
```

# Regression models

Regression models estimate associations and predictions. They do not, by themselves, provide evidence of causality.

## Linear regression

Purpose: Use linear regression to predict a continuous outcome from one or more predictors and estimate their associations. Regression coefficients should not be interpreted as causal effects without a suitable design.

```{r linear-regression}
data <- read_example("linearRegression.csv")

fit <- lm(
  race_time_min ~ training_volume_km_week + vo2max_ml_kg_min + age_years,
  data = data
)

summary(fit)
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(fit)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

## Binary logistic regression

Purpose: Use binary logistic regression to predict a two-category outcome and estimate effects as log-odds or odds ratios.

```{r binary-logistic-regression}
data <- read_example("logisticRegression.csv")
data$injury <- factor(data$injury, levels = c("no", "yes"))

fit <- glm(
  injury ~ training_load_au + previous_injury + recovery_score + age_years,
  data = data,
  family = binomial
)

summary(fit)
exp(coef(fit))
```

## Multinomial logistic regression

Purpose: Use multinomial logistic regression to predict an outcome with more than two unordered categories.

```{r multinomial-logistic-regression}
data <- read_example("multinomialRegression.csv")

if (has_pkg("nnet")) {
  data$treatment_outcome <- relevel(factor(data$treatment_outcome), ref = "completion")
  fit <- nnet::multinom(
    treatment_outcome ~ baseline_symptom_score + motivation_score + previous_therapy + age_years,
    data = data
  )
  summary(fit)
} else {
  message("Install nnet to run multinomial logistic regression.")
}
```

## Linear mixed model

Purpose: Use a linear mixed model for continuous outcomes when observations are clustered or repeated, allowing random effects such as participant-level intercepts.

```{r linear-mixed-model}
data <- read_example("linearMixedModel.csv")
data$participant <- factor(data$participant)
data$condition <- factor(data$condition)
data$team <- factor(data$team)

if (has_pkg("lme4") && has_pkg("lmerTest")) {
  fit <- lmerTest::lmer(score ~ condition + week + (1 | participant), data = data)
  summary(fit)

  if (has_pkg("performance")) {
    performance::r2_nakagawa(fit)
  }
} else {
  message("Install lme4 and lmerTest to run the linear mixed model.")
}
```

## Generalized linear mixed model

Purpose: Use a generalized linear mixed model for non-continuous outcomes, such as binary responses, when observations are clustered or repeated.

```{r generalized-linear-mixed-model}
data <- read_example("generalizedMixedModel.csv")
data$participant <- factor(data$participant)
data$condition <- factor(data$condition)
data$success_num <- ifelse(data$success %in% c("yes", "1", 1, "success"), 1, 0)

if (has_pkg("lme4")) {
  fit <- lme4::glmer(
    success_num ~ condition + week + (1 | participant),
    data = data,
    family = binomial
  )
  summary(fit)
  exp(lme4::fixef(fit))
} else {
  message("Install lme4 to run the generalized linear mixed model.")
}
```

## Ordinal mixed model

Purpose: Use an ordinal mixed model for ordered categorical outcomes collected in clustered or repeated-measures designs.

```{r ordinal-mixed-model}
data <- read_example("ordinalMixedModel.csv")
data$participant <- factor(data$participant)
data$condition <- factor(data$condition)
data$rating <- ordered(data$rating)

if (has_pkg("ordinal")) {
  fit <- ordinal::clmm(rating ~ condition + day + (1 | participant), data = data)
  summary(fit)
  exp(coef(fit))
} else {
  message("Install ordinal to run the ordinal mixed model.")
}
```

# Exploratory hypotheses

## Factor analysis

Purpose: Use factor analysis to identify latent dimensions that explain patterns of correlations among several observed variables.

```{r factor-analysis}
data <- read_example("factorAnalysis.csv")
items <- c(
  "stress_item_1", "stress_item_2", "stress_item_3",
  "mood_item_1", "mood_item_2", "mood_item_3",
  "sleep_item_1", "sleep_item_2"
)

if (has_pkg("psych")) {
  psych::fa.parallel(data[, items], fa = "fa")
  fit <- psych::fa(data[, items], nfactors = 3, rotate = "oblimin")
  print(fit$loadings, cutoff = .30)
} else {
  fit <- factanal(data[, items], factors = 3, rotation = "promax")
  print(fit)
}
```

## Network analysis

Purpose: Use network analysis to explore direct relations among several variables while keeping each variable as a node. The resulting edges should be interpreted as conditional associations, not as evidence of causality.

```{r network-analysis}
data <- read_example("networkAnalysis.csv")
items <- c("stress", "sleep_quality", "rumination", "mood", "motivation", "performance")

cor_mat <- cor(data[, items])
round(cor_mat, 2)

if (has_pkg("qgraph")) {
  qgraph::qgraph(
    cor_mat,
    layout = "spring",
    labels = items,
    theme = "colorblind"
  )
} else {
  message("Install qgraph to draw the network plot.")
}
```

## Cluster analysis

Purpose: Use cluster analysis to group people or objects into relatively homogeneous clusters based on similarity across variables.

```{r cluster-analysis}
data <- read_example("clusterAnalysis.csv")
variables <- c("training_load", "recovery_score", "sprint_time", "jump_height", "injury_count")

scaled <- scale(data[, variables])
d <- dist(scaled, method = "euclidean")
fit <- hclust(d, method = "ward.D2")
plot(fit)

clusters <- cutree(fit, k = 3)
table(clusters)

set.seed(1)
kmeans_fit <- kmeans(scaled, centers = 3, nstart = 25)
table(kmeans_fit$cluster)
```

## Multidimensional scaling

Purpose: Use multidimensional scaling to represent similarities or distances among objects in a low-dimensional visual space.

```{r multidimensional-scaling}
data <- read_example("multidimensionalScaling.csv")
variables <- c("calm", "joy", "anger", "sadness", "fear")

d <- dist(scale(data[, variables]))
fit <- cmdscale(d, k = 2, eig = TRUE)

plot(fit$points, xlab = "Dimension 1", ylab = "Dimension 2")
text(fit$points, labels = data$object, pos = 3)

if (has_pkg("MASS")) {
  nonmetric_fit <- MASS::isoMDS(d, k = 2)
  nonmetric_fit$stress
}
```

# Meta-analysis

## Odds ratio meta-analysis

Purpose: Use this meta-analysis when primary studies report binary events in two groups and the effect is summarized as an odds ratio.

```{r meta-odds-ratio}
data <- read_example("metaOddsRatio.csv")

if (has_pkg("metafor")) {
  data$non_events_treatment <- data$total_treatment - data$events_treatment
  data$non_events_control <- data$total_control - data$events_control

  es <- metafor::escalc(
    measure = "OR",
    ai = events_treatment,
    bi = non_events_treatment,
    ci = events_control,
    di = non_events_control,
    data = data
  )

  fit <- metafor::rma(yi, vi, mods = ~ moderator, data = es, method = "REML")
  summary(fit)
  metafor::forest(fit, slab = data$study_label)
} else {
  message("Install metafor to run this meta-analysis.")
}
```

## Correlation coefficient meta-analysis

Purpose: Use this meta-analysis to synthesize correlations across studies, typically after transforming correlations to Fisher's z scale.

```{r meta-correlation}
data <- read_example("metaCorrelation.csv")

if (has_pkg("metafor")) {
  es <- metafor::escalc(measure = "ZCOR", ri = correlation, ni = sample_size, data = data)
  fit <- metafor::rma(yi, vi, mods = ~ moderator, data = es, method = "REML")
  summary(fit)
  predict(fit, transf = metafor::transf.ztor)
  metafor::forest(fit, slab = data$study_label)
} else {
  message("Install metafor to run this meta-analysis.")
}
```

## Mean difference meta-analysis

Purpose: Use this meta-analysis when studies report group means, standard deviations, and sample sizes for a continuous outcome.

```{r meta-mean-difference}
data <- read_example("metaMeanDifferences.csv")

if (has_pkg("metafor")) {
  es <- metafor::escalc(
    measure = "SMD",
    m1i = mean_treatment,
    sd1i = sd_treatment,
    n1i = n_treatment,
    m2i = mean_control,
    sd2i = sd_control,
    n2i = n_control,
    data = data
  )

  fit <- metafor::rma(yi, vi, mods = ~ moderator, data = es, method = "REML")
  summary(fit)
  metafor::forest(fit, slab = data$study_label)
} else {
  message("Install metafor to run this meta-analysis.")
}
```

## Effect size meta-analysis

Purpose: Use this workflow when studies already provide an effect size with its variance or standard error.

```{r meta-effect-size}
data <- read_example("metaEffectSizes.csv")

if (has_pkg("metafor")) {
  fit <- metafor::rma(
    yi = effect_size,
    vi = variance,
    mods = ~ moderator,
    data = data,
    method = "REML"
  )

  summary(fit)
  metafor::forest(fit, slab = data$study_label)
  metafor::funnel(fit)
  metafor::regtest(fit, model = "rma")
} else {
  message("Install metafor to run this meta-analysis.")
}
```

## Fixed-effect meta-analysis

Purpose: Use a fixed-effect meta-analysis when the goal is to estimate one common effect assumed to be shared by all included studies.

```{r fixed-effect-meta-analysis}
data <- read_example("metaEffectSizes.csv")

if (has_pkg("metafor")) {
  fit <- metafor::rma(
    yi = effect_size,
    sei = se,
    data = data,
    method = "FE"
  )

  summary(fit)
  metafor::forest(fit, slab = data$study_label)
} else {
  message("Install metafor to run this meta-analysis.")
}
```

## Random-effects meta-analysis

Purpose: Use a random-effects meta-analysis when true effects are expected to vary across studies and the goal is to estimate the mean of that distribution.

```{r random-effects-meta-analysis}
data <- read_example("metaEffectSizes.csv")

if (has_pkg("metafor")) {
  fit <- metafor::rma(
    yi = effect_size,
    sei = se,
    data = data,
    method = "REML"
  )

  summary(fit)
  predict(fit)
  metafor::forest(fit, slab = data$study_label)
} else {
  message("Install metafor to run this meta-analysis.")
}
```

## Subgroup meta-analysis

Purpose: Use subgroup meta-analysis to examine whether pooled effects differ across categorical study characteristics.

```{r subgroup-meta-analysis}
data <- read_example("metaEffectSizes.csv")

if (has_pkg("metafor")) {
  fit <- metafor::rma(
    yi = effect_size,
    sei = se,
    mods = ~ moderator,
    data = data,
    method = "REML"
  )

  summary(fit)

  by(
    data,
    data$moderator,
    function(d) summary(metafor::rma(yi = effect_size, sei = se, data = d, method = "REML"))
  )
} else {
  message("Install metafor to run this meta-analysis.")
}
```

## Meta-regression

Purpose: Use meta-regression to examine whether study-level moderators explain variation in effect sizes across studies.

```{r meta-regression}
data <- read_example("metaEffectSizes.csv")

if (has_pkg("metafor")) {
  fit <- metafor::rma(
    yi = effect_size,
    sei = se,
    mods = ~ moderator,
    data = data,
    method = "REML"
  )

  summary(fit)
} else {
  message("Install metafor to run meta-regression.")
}
```

## Publication-bias diagnostics

Purpose: Use publication-bias diagnostics to inspect funnel plot asymmetry and possible small-study effects after fitting a meta-analysis model.

```{r publication-bias-diagnostics}
data <- read_example("metaEffectSizes.csv")

if (has_pkg("metafor")) {
  fit <- metafor::rma(
    yi = effect_size,
    sei = se,
    data = data,
    method = "REML"
  )

  metafor::funnel(fit)
  metafor::regtest(fit, model = "rma")
  metafor::trimfill(fit)
} else {
  message("Install metafor to run publication-bias diagnostics.")
}
```

## Proportion meta-analysis

Purpose: Use proportion meta-analysis to synthesize event proportions or prevalence estimates across studies.

```{r meta-proportion}
data <- read_example("metaProportion.csv")

if (has_pkg("metafor")) {
  es <- metafor::escalc(
    measure = "PLO",
    xi = event_frequency,
    ni = total_sample_size,
    data = data
  )

  fit <- metafor::rma(yi, vi, mods = ~ moderator, data = es, method = "REML")
  summary(fit)
  predict(fit, transf = metafor::transf.ilogit)
  metafor::forest(fit, slab = data$study_label)
} else {
  message("Install metafor to run this meta-analysis.")
}
```

# Machine learning regression

## Decision tree regression

Purpose: Use decision tree regression to predict a continuous outcome with transparent if-then decision rules.

```{r decision-tree-regression}
data <- read_example("decisionTreeRegression.csv")
model_data <- subset(data, select = -client)

if (has_pkg("rpart")) {
  set.seed(1)
  train_id <- sample(seq_len(nrow(model_data)), size = floor(0.70 * nrow(model_data)))

  fit <- rpart::rpart(
    outcome_symptom_score ~ motivation_score + baseline_distress_score + sessions_attended,
    data = model_data[train_id, ],
    method = "anova",
    control = rpart::rpart.control(cp = .01)
  )

  pred <- predict(fit, newdata = model_data[-train_id, ])
  actual <- model_data$outcome_symptom_score[-train_id]
  c(
    RMSE = sqrt(mean((pred - actual)^2)),
    MAE = mean(abs(pred - actual)),
    R2 = cor(pred, actual)^2
  )
  plot(fit)
  text(fit)
} else {
  message("Install rpart to run decision tree regression.")
}
```

## Random forest regression

Purpose: Use random forest regression to improve continuous-outcome prediction by averaging many decision trees and estimating variable importance.

```{r random-forest-regression}
data <- read_example("randomForestRegression.csv")
model_data <- subset(data, select = -athlete)

if (has_pkg("randomForest")) {
  set.seed(1)
  train_id <- sample(seq_len(nrow(model_data)), size = floor(0.70 * nrow(model_data)))

  fit <- randomForest::randomForest(
    race_time_min ~ .,
    data = model_data[train_id, ],
    ntree = 500,
    importance = TRUE
  )

  pred <- predict(fit, newdata = model_data[-train_id, ])
  actual <- model_data$race_time_min[-train_id]
  c(
    RMSE = sqrt(mean((pred - actual)^2)),
    MAE = mean(abs(pred - actual)),
    R2 = cor(pred, actual)^2
  )
  randomForest::importance(fit)
} else {
  message("Install randomForest to run random forest regression.")
}
```

## k-nearest neighbors regression

Purpose: Use k-nearest neighbors regression to predict a continuous outcome from the outcomes of similar cases in the predictor space.

```{r knn-regression}
data <- read_example("knnRegression.csv")
model_data <- subset(data, select = -participant)

if (has_pkg("caret")) {
  set.seed(1)
  ctrl <- caret::trainControl(method = "cv", number = 10)

  fit <- caret::train(
    wellbeing_score ~ sleep_hours + stress_score + physical_activity_min_week + social_support_score,
    data = model_data,
    method = "knn",
    trControl = ctrl,
    preProcess = c("center", "scale"),
    tuneLength = 10
  )

  fit
} else {
  message("Install caret to run k-nearest neighbors regression.")
}
```

# Machine learning classification

## Decision tree classifier

Purpose: Use a decision tree classifier to predict categorical group membership with interpretable decision rules.

```{r decision-tree-classifier}
data <- read_example("decisionTreeClassifier.csv")
data$class <- factor(data$class)
model_data <- subset(data, select = -participant)

if (has_pkg("rpart")) {
  set.seed(1)
  train_id <- sample(seq_len(nrow(model_data)), size = floor(0.70 * nrow(model_data)))

  fit <- rpart::rpart(
    class ~ .,
    data = model_data[train_id, ],
    method = "class"
  )

  pred <- predict(fit, newdata = model_data[-train_id, ], type = "class")
  table(Predicted = pred, Observed = model_data$class[-train_id])
  mean(pred == model_data$class[-train_id])

  # plot(fit)
  # text(fit)
} else {
  message("Install rpart to run decision tree classification.")
}
```

## Random forest classifier

Purpose: Use a random forest classifier to improve categorical prediction by combining many trees and evaluating variable importance.

```{r random-forest-classifier}
data <- read_example("randomForestClassifier.csv")
data$class <- factor(data$class)
model_data <- subset(data, select = -athlete)

if (has_pkg("randomForest")) {
  set.seed(1)
  train_id <- sample(seq_len(nrow(model_data)), size = floor(0.70 * nrow(model_data)))

  fit <- randomForest::randomForest(
    class ~ .,
    data = model_data[train_id, ],
    ntree = 500,
    importance = TRUE
  )

  pred <- predict(fit, newdata = model_data[-train_id, ])
  table(Predicted = pred, Observed = model_data$class[-train_id])
  mean(pred == model_data$class[-train_id])
  randomForest::importance(fit)
} else {
  message("Install randomForest to run random forest classification.")
}
```

## k-nearest neighbors classifier

Purpose: Use k-nearest neighbors classification to assign cases to categories based on the classes of similar observations.

```{r knn-classifier}
data <- read_example("knnClassifier.csv")
data$class <- factor(data$class)
model_data <- subset(data, select = -participant)

if (has_pkg("caret")) {
  set.seed(1)
  ctrl <- caret::trainControl(method = "cv", number = 10)

  fit <- caret::train(
    class ~ .,
    data = model_data,
    method = "knn",
    trControl = ctrl,
    preProcess = c("center", "scale"),
    tuneLength = 10
  )

  fit
} else {
  message("Install caret to run k-nearest neighbors classification.")
}
```

## Naive Bayes classifier

Purpose: Use a Naive Bayes classifier as a fast probabilistic model for categorical prediction, especially as a baseline model.

```{r naive-bayes-classifier}
data <- read_example("naiveBayes.csv")
data$class <- factor(data$class)
model_data <- subset(data, select = -student)

if (has_pkg("e1071")) {
  set.seed(1)
  train_id <- sample(seq_len(nrow(model_data)), size = floor(0.70 * nrow(model_data)))

  fit <- e1071::naiveBayes(class ~ ., data = model_data[train_id, ])
  pred <- predict(fit, newdata = model_data[-train_id, ])

  table(Predicted = pred, Observed = model_data$class[-train_id])
  mean(pred == model_data$class[-train_id])
} else {
  message("Install e1071 to run naive Bayes classification.")
}
```

# Principal component analysis

## Principal component analysis

Purpose: Use PCA to reduce several correlated continuous variables into fewer components that summarize the main sources of variance.

```{r principal-component-analysis}
data <- read_example("principalComponentAnalysis.csv")
variables <- c(
  "attention_accuracy",
  "working_memory",
  "processing_speed",
  "inhibition_score",
  "cognitive_flexibility",
  "verbal_memory",
  "spatial_reasoning"
)

vars <- data[, variables]
fit <- prcomp(vars, center = TRUE, scale. = TRUE)

summary(fit)
fit$rotation
biplot(fit, scale = 0)
```
